title: if Statements
subtitle: Make decisions in your code based on conditions
learning_objectives: Write a basic if statement with the correct syntax
Understand why the colon and indentation are required
Predict whether a block of code will execute based on a condition

sections.heading: What is an if Statement?
sections.content:
So far your code has always run line by line from top to bottom, doing the same thing every time. An if statement changes that — it lets your program make a decision and only run certain lines when a specific thing is true.

Think of it like a real-life decision: "If it is raining, take an umbrella." You only take the umbrella when the condition (raining) is true. If it's not raining, you skip that action entirely.

In Python it works the same way:

Three things to know before you write one:

1. Condition — the question Python checks. It must be something that is either True or False. For example: age >= 18 is a condition. Python checks it and gets either True or False.

2. The colon : — you must put a colon at the very end of the if line. This tells Python: "the instructions for this decision are coming up on the next lines." Forgetting the colon is the most common mistake beginners make.

3. The block — a block is a group of lines that belong together and run as a team. The lines inside an if statement form a block. Python knows which lines are in the block by looking at the indentation. Indentation simply means the empty space at the start of a line — the gap between the left edge of the screen and where the code begins. You create it by pressing the Tab key once (or pressing the spacebar 4 times) at the start of the line. Lines that have this gap are considered inside the block. Lines with no gap are outside it. Every line in the block must be indented by the same amount. Lines that are NOT indented are outside the block and always run, no matter what.
sections.code_example:
age = 20

if age >= 18:              # condition + colon
    print("You are an adult.")  # indented = inside the block
    print("You may vote.")      # also indented = also in the block

print("This line always runs.")  # NOT indented = outside the block
sections.code_output:
You are an adult.
You may vote.
This line always runs.

sections.heading: What Happens When the Condition is True or False
sections.content:
When Python reaches an if statement, it checks the condition:

If the condition is True → Python runs every line in the block (the indented lines), then continues to the rest of the code.

If the condition is False → Python completely skips the block (the indented lines) and jumps straight to the first unindented line below.

In both cases, any code that is NOT indented (outside the block) always runs — Python never skips those.

Look at the two examples below. The only difference is the value of score — but that one change decides which lines run.
sections.code_example:
# Example 1: condition is True — block runs
score = 75

if score >= 50:
    print("You passed!")   # this runs because 75 >= 50 is True

print("Quiz complete.")    # this always runs

# ---

# Example 2: condition is False — block is skipped
score = 45

if score >= 50:
    print("You passed!")   # this is SKIPPED because 45 >= 50 is False

print("Quiz complete.")    # this still runs
sections.code_output:
You passed!
Quiz complete.
Quiz complete.
sections.note: A block is just lines of code that are pushed to the right with the Tab key. They belong to the if statement. If the condition is False, the whole block gets skipped.

exercises.title: Age Check
exercises.prompt: Write an if statement that checks whether a person is old enough to drive. Set age = 16, then print "You can drive!" if age is greater than or equal to 16. Note: Remember the colon after the condition (if age >= 16:) and indent the print() line by 4 spaces.
exercises.starter_code:
age = 16

# Write your if statement below
hints: Use >= to check 'greater than or equal to'
Don't forget the colon after the condition: if age >= 16:

exercises.title: Score Check
exercises.prompt: Set score = 75. Write an if statement that prints "Great job!" when the score is above 70, and also always prints "Thanks for taking the quiz." after the if block. Note: The second print() should NOT be indented — it lives outside the if block and always runs.
exercises.starter_code:
score = 75

# Write your if statement here

# Always print this line
exercises.solution:
score = 75

if score > 70:
    print("Great job!")

print("Thanks for taking the quiz.")
hints: The second print should be at the same indentation level as the if keyword, not inside the block

tests.message: Output should always contain the closing message

quiz.question: What are the two things required after the condition in an if statement?
options: A colon and indented code below
A semicolon and a new line
quiz.explanation: Python requires a colon at the end of the if line and 4-space indentation for every line in the block. Without either one, you get a SyntaxError.

quiz.question:
Given x = 3, what does the following code print?

if x > 5:
    print("big")
print("done")
quiz.explanation: Because x = 3 is not greater than 5, the indented block is skipped. The unindented print("done") always runs, so only 'done' is printed.

quiz.question: What happens if you forget the colon at the end of an if line?
options: Python ignores it and runs fine
The block runs regardless of the condition
quiz.explanation: The colon is required. If you leave it out, Python immediately raises a SyntaxError and your code won't run at all.

quiz.question: Which of these is a valid condition inside an if statement?
quiz.explanation: == is the comparison operator that checks if two values are equal. A single = is assignment (storing a value), not comparison. Python has no === operator.

quiz.question: How does Python know which lines belong inside an if block?
options: They are wrapped in curly braces { }
They are indented (pushed to the right) with spaces or a tab
quiz.explanation: Python uses indentation — the empty space at the start of a line — to group lines into a block. Lines at the same indentation level belong to the same block. This is unique to Python; many other languages use curly braces instead.

completion_message: Excellent work! You now know how to write an if statement, use the colon, and indent your code correctly. Next up: handling the False case with else and elif.
